Musical artist

**Rickie Lee Jones** (born November 8, 1954) is an American singer, musician, and songwriter. Over the course of a career that spans five decades and 15 studio albums, she has recorded in various musical styles including [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), and [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz").[[1]](#cite_note-allmusic-1) A two-time [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") winner (from eight nominations),[[2]](#cite_note-Gram-2) Jones was listed at No. 30 on [VH1](/wiki/VH1 "VH1")'s [100 Greatest Women in Rock & Roll](/wiki/The_Greatest_(VH1_TV_series) "The Greatest (VH1 TV series)") in 1999.[[3]](#cite_note-rockonthenet-3) [AllMusic](/wiki/AllMusic "AllMusic") stated: "Few singer/songwriters are as individual and eclectic as Rickie Lee Jones, a vocalist with an expressive and smoky instrument, and a composer who can weave jazz, folk, and R&B into songs with a distinct pop sensibility."[[1]](#cite_note-allmusic-1)

She released her [self-titled debut album](/wiki/Rickie_Lee_Jones_(album) "Rickie Lee Jones (album)") in 1979, to critical and commercial success. It peaked at No. 3 on the U.S. [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), and spawned the hit single "[Chuck E.'s in Love](/wiki/Chuck_E.%27s_in_Love "Chuck E.'s in Love")", which peaked at No. 4 on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100").[[4]](#cite_note-billboard-4) The album went [platinum](/wiki/Music_recording_certification "Music recording certification") later that year,[[5]](#cite_note-RIAA-5) and earned Jones four Grammy Award nominations in [1980](/wiki/22nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "22nd Annual Grammy Awards"), including [Best New Artist](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_New_Artist "Grammy Award for Best New Artist"), which she won.[[2]](#cite_note-Gram-2) Her second album, [*Pirates*](/wiki/Pirates_(Rickie_Lee_Jones_album) "Pirates (Rickie Lee Jones album)"), followed in 1981 to further critical and commercial success; it peaked at No. 5 on the *Billboard* 200, went [gold](/wiki/Music_recording_certification "Music recording certification"),[[5]](#cite_note-RIAA-5) and ranked No. 49 on [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR")'s list of the 150 Greatest Albums Made by Women in 2017.[[6]](#cite_note-npr-6)

Her third album, *[The Magazine](/wiki/The_Magazine_(album) "The Magazine (album)")*, appeared in 1984 before Jones took a brief hiatus from recording. Her fourth album, *[Flying Cowboys](/wiki/Flying_Cowboys "Flying Cowboys")*, was released in 1989 and later went gold.[[5]](#cite_note-RIAA-5) Jones won her second Grammy Award in [1990](/wiki/32nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "32nd Annual Grammy Awards") for "[Makin' Whoopee](/wiki/Makin%27_Whoopee "Makin' Whoopee")", a duet with [Dr. John](/wiki/Dr._John "Dr. John"), this time in the category of [Best Jazz Vocal Performance, Duo or Group](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Jazz_Vocal_Performance,_Duo_or_Group "Grammy Award for Best Jazz Vocal Performance, Duo or Group").[[2]](#cite_note-Gram-2) Jones' seventh Grammy Award nomination followed in [2001](/wiki/43rd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "43rd Annual Grammy Awards") in the category of [Best Traditional Pop Vocal Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Traditional_Pop_Vocal_Album "Grammy Award for Best Traditional Pop Vocal Album") for her album *[It's Like This](/wiki/It%27s_Like_This "It's Like This")* (2000).[[2]](#cite_note-Gram-2) In 2021, Jones released her memoir *Last Chance Texaco: Chronicles of an American Troubadour*.[[7]](#cite_note-book1-7) Her 15th studio album, *[Pieces of Treasure](/wiki/Pieces_of_Treasure "Pieces of Treasure")*, was released in 2023 and earned Jones her eighth Grammy Award nomination, for Best Traditional Pop Vocal Album.[[8]](#cite_note-grammys2024-8)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rickie_Lee_Jones&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Jones was born the third of four children to Richard and Bettye Jones, on the north side of [Chicago, Illinois](/wiki/Chicago,_Illinois "Chicago, Illinois"), on November 8, 1954.[[9]](#cite_note-billboardbio-9) She was named after her father, who was a singer, songwriter, painter, and trumpet player. Her mother, Bettye, was raised in orphanages around [Mansfield, Ohio](/wiki/Mansfield,_Ohio "Mansfield, Ohio").[[10]](#cite_note-10) She has a brother, Daniel, and two sisters, Janet Adele and Pamela Jo.[[11]](#cite_note-Timothy_White_1979,_p._20-11) Her paternal grandfather, [Frank "Peg Leg" Jones](/wiki/List_of_vaudeville_performers:_A%E2%80%93K "List of vaudeville performers: A–K"), and her grandmother, Myrtle Lee, were vaudevillians based in Chicago.[[12]](#cite_note-Peg_Leg-12) A singer, dancer, and comedian, Peg Leg Jones's routine consisted of singing and accompanying himself on ukulele, soft shoe dance, acrobatics, and comedy.[[12]](#cite_note-Peg_Leg-12)

Jones lived in [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), from age 4 to 14.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rickie_Lee_Jones&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early years: 1975–1982

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rickie_Lee_Jones&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years: 1975–1982")]

At the age of 21, Jones began singing traditional jazz and original compositions in bars and coffee houses in [Venice](/wiki/Venice,_Los_Angeles "Venice, Los Angeles"), California. There she met Alfred Johnson, a piano player and songwriter, with whom she wrote "Weasel and the White Boys Cool", and "Company", which would later appear on Jones's debut album.[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 1977, Jones met [Tom Waits](/wiki/Tom_Waits "Tom Waits") at [The Troubadour](/wiki/The_Troubadour_(Los_Angeles) "The Troubadour (Los Angeles)").[[15]](#cite_note-independent1-15) They dated for about two years, before splitting in 1979.[[15]](#cite_note-independent1-15)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-audio-openclipart.svg.png)

["Chuck E.'s in Love"](/wiki/File:Rickie_Lee_Jones-Chuck_E.ogg "File:Rickie Lee Jones-Chuck E.ogg")

20-second sample

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

*[Rickie Lee Jones](/wiki/Rickie_Lee_Jones_(album) "Rickie Lee Jones (album)")* was released in March 1979 and became a critical and commercial hit, buoyed by the success of the jazz-flavored single "[Chuck E.'s in Love](/wiki/Chuck_E.%27s_in_Love "Chuck E.'s in Love")", which hit No. 4 on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100"), and featured an accompanying [music video](/wiki/Music_video "Music video"). The song was occasioned by her friend, Chuck E. Weiss, telephoning her and Tom Waits, all three of them close friends at the time, in the fall of 1977 to tell them that he had fallen in love.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The album, which included guest appearances by [Dr. John](/wiki/Dr._John "Dr. John"), [Randy Newman](/wiki/Randy_Newman "Randy Newman"), and [Michael McDonald](/wiki/Michael_McDonald_(singer) "Michael McDonald (singer)"), reached No. 3 on the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), went Platinum,[[5]](#cite_note-RIAA-5) and produced another Top 40 hit with "Young Blood" (No. 40) in late 1979.

Her appearance – as an unknown (one month after her debut record had been released) – on *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")* on April 7, 1979, sparked an overnight sensation. She performed "Chuck E.'s in Love" and "Coolsville".[[17]](#cite_note-17) Jones was covered by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine on her very first professional show, in Boston, and they dubbed her "The Duchess of Coolsville". Touring after the album's release, she played [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall") on July 22, 1979. Members of her group included native New York guitarist [Buzz Feiten](/wiki/Buzz_Feiten "Buzz Feiten"), who was featured on the album and would appear in her recorded works for over a decade. Following her first-ever performances in the spring/summer of 1979, Jones appeared on the cover of *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine. Photographed by [Annie Leibovitz](/wiki/Annie_Leibovitz "Annie Leibovitz"), the cover image showed Jones posing in a crouched stance, wearing a black bra and a white beret.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Jones secured four nominations at the [22nd Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/22nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "22nd Annual Grammy Awards"): [Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year") and [Best Pop Vocal Performance, Female](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Female_Pop_Vocal_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance") for "Chuck E.'s in Love"; [Best Rock Vocal Performance, Female](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Female_Rock_Vocal_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Female Rock Vocal Performance") for "The Last Chance Texaco"; and [Best New Artist](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_New_Artist "Grammy Award for Best New Artist"), which she won. The album also earned a nomination for [Best Engineered Recording - Non-Classical](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Engineered_Album,_Non-Classical "Grammy Award for Best Engineered Album, Non-Classical"), credited to Tom Knox.[[19]](#cite_note-tomknox-19)

In 1980, [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola") asked Jones to collaborate with Waits on his upcoming film *[One from the Heart](/wiki/One_from_the_Heart "One from the Heart")*, but she balked, citing their recent breakup in late 1979.[[15]](#cite_note-independent1-15) Coppola argued that the duet would be perfect for the film, since the two main characters in the film are separated, and he asked her to reconsider. Waits ultimately sang with country pop star [Crystal Gayle](/wiki/Crystal_Gayle "Crystal Gayle").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 1981, Jones released her second album, *[Pirates](/wiki/Pirates_(Rickie_Lee_Jones_album) "Pirates (Rickie Lee Jones album)")*, which received high marks from critics and was a commercial success. The album reached No. 5 on the *Billboard* 200, and soon achieved Gold certification.[[5]](#cite_note-RIAA-5) *Rolling Stone* remained a fervent supporter of Jones, with a second cover feature in 1981;[[21]](#cite_note-21) the magazine also included a glowing five-star review of *Pirates*.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The single "A Lucky Guy" became the only *Billboard* Hot 100 hit from the album, peaking at No. 64, but "Pirates (So Long Lonely Avenue)" and "Woody and Dutch on the Slow Train to Peking" became minor Top 40 hits on the *Billboard* Mainstream Rock chart.[[4]](#cite_note-billboard-4)

Jones left New York for San Francisco where she befriended [Robin Williams](/wiki/Robin_Williams "Robin Williams"). In Los Angeles, she recorded the EP *[Girl at Her Volcano](/wiki/Girl_at_Her_Volcano "Girl at Her Volcano")*, producing the record herself and drawing the cover art. It was released as a 10" record in 1983, featuring a mix of live and studio cover versions of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz_standards "Jazz standards") and [pop standards](/wiki/Pop_standards "Pop standards"), as well as one Jones original, "Hey, Bub", which was originally written for *Pirates*. Jones then relocated to Paris.[[7]](#cite_note-book1-7): 332

### Period of transition: 1983–1989

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rickie_Lee_Jones&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Period of transition: 1983–1989")]

In 1983, Jones lived in Paris for four months, writing new material for her third full-length solo album, *[The Magazine](/wiki/The_Magazine_(album) "The Magazine (album)")*, released in September 1984. *The Magazine* was produced by Jones and [James Newton Howard](/wiki/James_Newton_Howard "James Newton Howard") and included a three-song suite, subtitled "Rorschachs", which featured multi-tracked vocals and minimalist synth patterns.[[23]](#cite_note-RLJ_Magazine-23) The lead single, "The Real End", reached No. 82 on the *Billboard* Hot 100 in 1984.[[4]](#cite_note-billboard-4)

Jones took a four-year break from her recording schedule, largely attributed to the deaths of her mentor Bob Regher as well as her father, Richard Loris Jones, that same year.[[24]](#cite_note-24) During this period, she replaced [Shirley Jones](/wiki/Shirley_Jones "Shirley Jones") for the role of the Fairy Godmother in [Filmation's](/wiki/Filmation "Filmation") 1987 film, *[Pinocchio and the Emperor of the Night](/wiki/Pinocchio_and_the_Emperor_of_the_Night "Pinocchio and the Emperor of the Night")*, including a performance of the original song "Love is the Light Inside Your Heart".[[25]](#cite_note-25)

After a successful tour of Norway and Sweden, and then opening for [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles") in Israel with [Michael Lang](/wiki/Michael_Lang_(producer) "Michael Lang (producer)") managing her, she returned to the US, signed to [Geffen Records](/wiki/Geffen_Records "Geffen Records") by [Gary Gersh](/wiki/Gary_Gersh "Gary Gersh"), who teamed her with [Steely Dan](/wiki/Steely_Dan "Steely Dan")'s [Walter Becker](/wiki/Walter_Becker "Walter Becker") for her long-awaited fourth album. In September 1988, the two of them began work on *[Flying Cowboys](/wiki/Flying_Cowboys "Flying Cowboys")*. The album was released in September 1989, and produced two hits: "Satellites", which hit No. 1 on the new Adult radio format; and "[The Horses](/wiki/The_Horses "The Horses")", co-written with Becker. The latter song was covered by [Kenny Loggins](/wiki/Kenny_Loggins "Kenny Loggins"), and also featured in the movie *[Jerry Maguire](/wiki/Jerry_Maguire "Jerry Maguire")* (1996). "The Horses" also became an Australian No. 1 hit single for [Daryl Braithwaite](/wiki/Daryl_Braithwaite "Daryl Braithwaite") when he covered it in 1991.[[26]](#cite_note-26) *Flying Cowboys* made the US Top 40, reaching No. 39 on the *Billboard* 200, with the college radio hit "Satellites" making it to No. 23 on the *Billboard* Modern Rock Tracks chart.[[4]](#cite_note-billboard-4) The album was certified Gold in 1997.[[5]](#cite_note-RIAA-5)

Her 1988 collaboration with [Rob Wasserman](/wiki/Rob_Wasserman "Rob Wasserman"), ["Autumn Leaves"](/wiki/Autumn_Leaves_(1945_song) "Autumn Leaves (1945 song)") on his album *Duets*, earned Jones a [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") nomination for [Best Jazz Vocal Performance, Female](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Jazz_Vocal_Performance,_Female "Grammy Award for Best Jazz Vocal Performance, Female") in [1989](/wiki/31st_Annual_Grammy_Awards "31st Annual Grammy Awards").[[2]](#cite_note-Gram-2) Her duet with [Dr. John](/wiki/Dr._John "Dr. John"), a cover of "[Makin' Whoopee](/wiki/Makin%27_Whoopee "Makin' Whoopee")", won her second Grammy Award in [1990](/wiki/32nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "32nd Annual Grammy Awards"), this time in the category of [Best Jazz Vocal Collaboration](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Jazz_Vocal_Performance,_Duo_or_Group "Grammy Award for Best Jazz Vocal Performance, Duo or Group").[[2]](#cite_note-Gram-2)

### Experimentation and change: 1990–2001

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rickie_Lee_Jones&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Experimentation and change: 1990–2001")]

| --- | --- |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Rickie-Lee-Jones.jpg/250px-Rickie-Lee-Jones.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rickie-Lee-Jones.jpg)

Jones in concert

Following a tour with [Lyle Lovett](/wiki/Lyle_Lovett "Lyle Lovett"), Jones enlisted [David Was](/wiki/David_Was "David Was") to produce her first album of jazz covers. Her producer chose an innovative approach and she agreed, thus the Argentinian flavored *[Pop Pop](/wiki/Pop_Pop "Pop Pop")*, set the pace for what would become a habit with Jones - mixing styles of jazz and pop and not staying in one genre for a whole recording: [Tin Pan Alley](/wiki/Tin_Pan_Alley "Tin Pan Alley") to [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix"). The album, released in September 1991, was a hit on the *Billboard* Contemporary Jazz Albums, peaking at No. 8, but became her least commercially successful record yet, reaching No. 121 on the *Billboard* 200.[[4]](#cite_note-billboard-4)

Soon after, [The Orb](/wiki/The_Orb "The Orb") issued "[Little Fluffy Clouds](/wiki/Little_Fluffy_Clouds "Little Fluffy Clouds")", featuring a [sampled](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") Jones interview. However, Jones' record company objected to the unauthorized use of her voice and pursued the issue in the court system. In 1992 she toured extensively with [Rob Wasserman](/wiki/Rob_Wasserman "Rob Wasserman"), with whom she had collaborated in the mid-1980s.

Her swan song for Geffen Records was *[Traffic from Paradise](/wiki/Traffic_from_Paradise "Traffic from Paradise")*, released in September 1993. The album was slightly more successful than its predecessor, reaching No. 111 on the *Billboard* 200, and was notable for its collaboration with [Leo Kottke](/wiki/Leo_Kottke "Leo Kottke"), its musical diversity, and a cover of [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie")'s "[Rebel Rebel](/wiki/Rebel_Rebel "Rebel Rebel")", which was originally planned to be the title track for the Oscar-winning film *[Boys Don't Cry](/wiki/Boys_Don%27t_Cry_(1999_film) "Boys Don't Cry (1999 film)").* Before his death, Bowie was quoted as saying that this version was his favorite cover of his work.

Throughout this period, her songs were featured in a number of films and television series, including *[House M.D.](/wiki/House_(TV_series) "House (TV series)")*, *[Thirtysomething](/wiki/Thirtysomething_(TV_series) "Thirtysomething (TV series)")*, *[Frankie and Johnny](/wiki/Frankie_and_Johnny_(1991_film) "Frankie and Johnny (1991 film)")*, *[When a Man Loves a Woman](/wiki/When_a_Man_Loves_a_Woman_(film) "When a Man Loves a Woman (film)")*, *[Jerry Maguire](/wiki/Jerry_Maguire "Jerry Maguire")*, *[Friends with Money](/wiki/Friends_with_Money "Friends with Money")* and the French film *[Subway](/wiki/Subway_(film) "Subway (film)")*.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Jones sang a duet with [Lyle Lovett](/wiki/Lyle_Lovett "Lyle Lovett") on "North Dakota" for his 1992 album *[Joshua Judges Ruth](/wiki/Joshua_Judges_Ruth "Joshua Judges Ruth")* and has also sung on albums by [John Mellencamp](/wiki/John_Mellencamp "John Mellencamp"), [Leo Kottke](/wiki/Leo_Kottke "Leo Kottke") and [Arlo Guthrie](/wiki/Arlo_Guthrie "Arlo Guthrie").

Jones' first solo shows in 1994 paved the way for her acoustic album *[Naked Songs](/wiki/Naked_Songs_%E2%80%93_Live_and_Acoustic "Naked Songs – Live and Acoustic")*, released in September 1995 through a one-off deal with [Reprise Records](/wiki/Reprise_Records "Reprise Records"). The album, which reached No. 121 on the *Billboard* 200, featured acoustic re-workings of Jones classics and album material, but no new songs. The same year, Jones produced [Leo Kottke](/wiki/Leo_Kottke "Leo Kottke")'s *[Peculiaroso](/wiki/Peculiaroso "Peculiaroso")* album.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Emphasizing her experimentation and change, Jones embraced electronic music for *[Ghostyhead](/wiki/Ghostyhead "Ghostyhead")*, released on Reprise Records in June 1997. The album, a collaboration with [Rick Boston](/wiki/Rick_Boston "Rick Boston") (both are credited with production and with twenty-one instruments in common), found Jones employing beats, loops, and electronic rhythms, and also showcased Jones' connection with the [trip hop](/wiki/Trip_hop "Trip hop") movement of the mid-to-late 1990s. Despite critical acclaim, it did not meet with commercial success, peaking at No. 159 on the *Billboard* 200.[[4]](#cite_note-billboard-4)

Jones' second album of cover versions, *[It's Like This](/wiki/It%27s_Like_This "It's Like This")*, was released on the independent record label [Artemis Records](/wiki/Artemis_Records "Artemis Records") in September 2000. The album included cover versions of material by artists including [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), [Steely Dan](/wiki/Steely_Dan "Steely Dan"), [Marvin Gaye](/wiki/Marvin_Gaye "Marvin Gaye"), and the [Gershwin](/wiki/Gershwin "Gershwin") brothers. It made it onto three *Billboard* charts – No.148 on the *Billboard* 200, No. 10 on Top Internet Albums, and No. 42 on Top Independent Albums. The album also secured Jones another Grammy Award nomination for [Best Traditional Pop Vocal Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Traditional_Pop_Vocal_Album "Grammy Award for Best Traditional Pop Vocal Album"). Her cover art design (shared with a staff artist but never credited to Jones) won numerous awards and is in the book Best Album Covers.

In November 2001, Artemis issued a release of archival material titled *Live at Red Rocks* with cover art by a young fan who had recently died in a swimming accident. Her parents brought her drawing of flying horses to one of Jones' shows in Oregon, and Jones used it for this live release.

### Artistic renaissance: 2002–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rickie_Lee_Jones&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Artistic renaissance: 2002–present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Rickie_Lee_Jones_on_Legacy_Stage.jpg/250px-Rickie_Lee_Jones_on_Legacy_Stage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rickie_Lee_Jones_on_Legacy_Stage.jpg)

Jones performing on the Legacy Stage on June 15, 2007

After *Ghostyhead*, Jones largely retired from public view, tending her garden and bringing up her teenage daughter Charlotte.[[29]](#cite_note-HiltonNYMag-29)

Released on the independent label [V2](/wiki/V2_Records "V2 Records") in October 2003, *[The Evening of My Best Day](/wiki/The_Evening_of_My_Best_Day "The Evening of My Best Day")* featured influences from jazz, [Celtic folk](/wiki/Celtic_folk "Celtic folk"), blues, R&B, rock, and [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), and spawned a successful and lengthy spurt of touring. The album peaked at No. 189 on the *Billboard* 200.[[4]](#cite_note-billboard-4) She invited punk bass icon [Mike Watt](/wiki/Mike_Watt "Mike Watt") (the Minutemen, Iggy Pop) to perform on "It Takes You There", while "Ugly Man" was a direct aim at the [George Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") 'regime' evoking, with an anthem-like [Hugh Masekela](/wiki/Hugh_Masekela "Hugh Masekela") arrangement, what she termed "the [Black Panther](/wiki/Black_Panther_Party "Black Panther Party") horns", and calling for "revolution, everywhere that you're not looking, revolution."[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Renewed interest in Jones led to the three-disc anthology *Duchess of Coolsville: An Anthology*, released through reissue specialists [Rhino](/wiki/Rhino_Entertainment "Rhino Entertainment") in June 2005.[[31]](#cite_note-31) A lavish package designed by Lee Cantelon, the alphabetically arranged release featured album songs, live material, covers, and demos, and featured essays by Jones as well as various collaborators, as well as tributes from artists including [Randy Newman](/wiki/Randy_Newman "Randy Newman"), [Walter Becker](/wiki/Walter_Becker "Walter Becker"), [Quincy Jones](/wiki/Quincy_Jones "Quincy Jones"), and [Tori Amos](/wiki/Tori_Amos "Tori Amos").[[32]](#cite_note-rickieleejonesbio-32)

Also in 2005, Jones was invited to take part in her boyfriend and collaborator Lee Cantelon's music version of his book *The Words*, a book of the words of Christ, set into simple chapters and themes. Cantelon's idea was to have various artists recite the text over primal rock music, but Jones elected to try something that had never been done, to improvise her own impression of the texts, melody and lyric, in stream of consciousness sessions, rather than read Jesus' words. The sessions were recorded at an artist's loft on Exposition Boulevard in Culver City. When Cantelon could no longer finish the project, Jones picked it up as her own record and hired Rob Schnaf to finish the production at [Sunset Sound](/wiki/Sunset_Sound "Sunset Sound") in 2007, and the result was *[The Sermon on Exposition Boulevard](/wiki/The_Sermon_on_Exposition_Boulevard "The Sermon on Exposition Boulevard")*, released on the independent [New West Records](/wiki/New_West_Records "New West Records") in February 2007. It included "Circle in the Sand", recorded for the soundtrack to the film *[Friends with Money](/wiki/Friends_with_Money "Friends with Money")* (2006), for which Jones also cut "Hillbilly Song".[[32]](#cite_note-rickieleejonesbio-32) *The Sermon on Exposition Boulevard* debuted at No. 158 on the *Billboard* 200 and No. 12 on the Top Independent Albums tally. Writer Ann Powers included this on her list of Grammy-worthy CDs for 2007.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

For her next project, *[Balm in Gilead](/wiki/Balm_in_Gilead_(album) "Balm in Gilead (album)")* (2009), Jones opted to finish half-written songs dating back as far as 1986 ("Wild Girl") as well as include new ones (the 2008-penned "The Gospel of Carlos, Norman and Smith", "Bonfires"). The album also included a new recording of "The Moon Is Made of Gold", a song written by her father Richard Loris Jones in 1954. [Ben Harper](/wiki/Ben_Harper "Ben Harper"), [Victoria Williams](/wiki/Victoria_Williams "Victoria Williams"), [Jon Brion](/wiki/Jon_Brion "Jon Brion"), [Alison Krauss](/wiki/Alison_Krauss "Alison Krauss") and the late [Vic Chesnutt](/wiki/Vic_Chesnutt "Vic Chesnutt") all made contributions to the album.[[32]](#cite_note-rickieleejonesbio-32)

In May 2010, Jones performed at the [Sydney Opera House](/wiki/Sydney_Opera_House "Sydney Opera House") as part of the [Vivid Live](/wiki/Vivid_Live "Vivid Live") festival.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Jones also served as the narrator of [Cam Archer](/wiki/Cam_Archer "Cam Archer")'s 2010 film *[Shit Year](/wiki/Shit_Year "Shit Year")*.[[35]](#cite_note-variety-35)

On September 18, 2012, Jones released *[The Devil You Know](/wiki/The_Devil_You_Know_(Rickie_Lee_Jones_album) "The Devil You Know (Rickie Lee Jones album)")* on Fantasy/Concord Records.[[36]](#cite_note-36) *The Devil You Know* includes a collection of covers produced by Ben Harper, including a solo version of "[Sympathy for the Devil](/wiki/Sympathy_for_the_Devil "Sympathy for the Devil")". Shortly afterward she left Los Angeles and moved to New Orleans.[[37]](#cite_note-TIDAL_RLJ_Interview-37)

In 2015, Jones released her album *[The Other Side of Desire](/wiki/The_Other_Side_of_Desire "The Other Side of Desire")*, and the single "Jimmy Choos" which references the [shoe brand](/wiki/Jimmy_Choo_Ltd "Jimmy Choo Ltd").[[38]](#cite_note-38) A documentary film, *Rickie Lee Jones: The Other Side of Desire*, on the making of the album, was also released.[[39]](#cite_note-39) It was her first album of all new original material since *[Balm in Gilead](/wiki/Balm_in_Gilead_(album) "Balm in Gilead (album)")* six years earlier.

In 2019, Jones released a single of the Paul Rodgers/Simon Kirke song, "[Bad Company](/wiki/Bad_Company_(song) "Bad Company (song)")", followed by her album *[Kicks](/wiki/Kicks_(Rickie_Lee_Jones_album) "Kicks (Rickie Lee Jones album)")* which included "Bad Company" and cover versions of many other songs. In June of that year, she played at the [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival").[[40]](#cite_note-Glastonbury_2019-40)

In 2021, Jones's memoir *Last Chance Texaco: Chronicles of an American Troubadour* was released by Grove Press.[[7]](#cite_note-book1-7)[[41]](#cite_note-41) Simon and Schuster bought the audio book rights.

## Activism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rickie_Lee_Jones&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Activism")]

In 2001, Jones was the organizer of the web community "Furniture for the People", which was founded by Jones to promote "peace and activism", and to act as a public forum. Members of the group engage in activities such as [gardening](/wiki/Gardening "Gardening"), [social activism](/wiki/Activism "Activism"), [bootleg exchange](/wiki/Bootleg_recording "Bootleg recording") and [left-wing politics](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### Grammy Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rickie_Lee_Jones&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Grammy Awards")]

The [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") are awarded annually by [The Recording Academy](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy") of the United States for outstanding achievements in the music industry. Often considered the highest music honor, the awards were established in 1958.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Jones has won two awards, from eight nominations.
